paraphrase: Tormund warns that the Pink Letter might be full of lies, seeding doubt about whether it should be taken at face value.
It's hard to believe Stannis is really dead because his story isn't over -- his whole ADWD arc builds up to a battle for Winterfell that hasn't happened on the page yet.
George R.R. Martin has said audiences will see Stannis's battle for Winterfell in the next book, which the video argues would make it strange for Stannis to simply die off-screen via a letter.
Stannis's army is described as snowbound and starving in a village near Winterfell, and the Boltons send the Freys out to attack him -- the dire situation the beacon plan has to respond to.
Despite his army's dire situation, Stannis is confident, telling his men “let them come.”
Stannis says he will turn “the ground” to his “advantage,” which the video reads as a hint toward a specific battle plan.
Early in the book, a story is recalled about an island lighthouse where people would sometimes turn off the real light and create a false beacon elsewhere to lure ships to danger.
Stannis's camp has a watchtower with a “beacon fire” that burns day and night, and the camp sits next to frozen lakes.
Stannis's men fish the frozen lakes by cutting holes in the ice, and the ice is dangerous enough that some men have fallen through into the water.

The video synthesizes these details into a theory: if Stannis puts out the real watchtower beacon and lights a false one out on the frozen lake, he could lure the attacking Freys onto dangerous ice and break it with catapults to drown them -- this is what he means by turning “the ground” to his “advantage,” and the video says the hints make this seem almost certain.

spoken_quote: you may hear that I am dead
paraphrase: If Stannis does win this battle, the video asks why the Pink Letter would say he's dead, noting that in his last-seen chapter Stannis specifically says “you may hear that I am dead,” which some fans read as him planning to fake his own death.
The Manderlys are coming to the fight “separately” from the Freys because the two houses have secretly been fighting each other -- at one point Wyman Manderly serves meat pies made out of Freys.
Wyman Manderly is planning to betray the Boltons and has made a secret deal to support Stannis.

The video theorizes that instead of fighting each other, Stannis and the Manderlys could work together: after Stannis defeats the Freys, Wyman could return to Winterfell and falsely tell the Boltons Stannis was defeated, using Stannis's sword Lightbringer (which Ramsay claims to have in the Pink Letter) as proof.
With the Boltons believing him dead, Stannis could secretly use the low visibility of a snowstorm to sneak up and attack Winterfell while Ramsay is unprepared.

Under this scenario Ramsay would just be angry that Jon sent Mance to steal his bride, which the video says explains why the Pink Letter threatens Jon and demands Jeyne and Theon back, since they'd likely seek refuge with Jon at the Wall.
The Pink Letter's tone fits Ramsay's personality -- angry and cruel.
Ramsay repeatedly calls Jon “bastard” in the letter, which the video attributes to Ramsay's own insecurity about being a bastard himself.
Ramsay is described as “cunning” and manipulative, and the video suggests he might be trying to provoke Jon into doing something stupid.

spoken_quote: tightly rolled and sealed with a button of hard pink wax
paraphrase: Ramsay's two earlier threatening letters (to Asha Greyjoy and to Jon) are both described in matching, specific detail: “tightly rolled and sealed with a button of hard pink wax,” written in blood that looks brown, in huge spiky handwriting, and signed by Ramsay along with allies Lady Dustin, Cerwyn, four Ryswells, and one Umber.
verbatim: “My lady.” The maester’s voice was anxious, as it always was when he spoke to her. “A bird from Barrowton.” He thrust the parchment at her as if he could not wait to be rid of it. It was tightly rolled and sealed with a button of hard pink wax.

paraphrase: By contrast, the Pink Letter is described differently -- it has only a “smear” of pink wax, with no mention of brown ink or huge spiky handwriting, and is signed by Ramsay alone rather than by his usual allies.
The letter's demand for Stannis's “bride” Jeyne and his “Reek” Theon makes sense for Ramsay, as does wanting Stannis's family as political prisoners.
But the letter also demands the “wildling princess” Val and her baby nephew, and the video questions why Ramsay would want these wildlings, or how he would even know Val's baby nephew exists.
The video allows that if Ramsay really did capture Mance, he could have interrogated him to learn these wildling-related details.
Still, it's surprising that so much of the Pink Letter is focused on Mance and the wildlings, and the video asks whether Ramsay is really just that angry about Mance rescuing Jeyne.
The letter refers to the Night's Watch as “black crows,” a term the video says is almost only used by wildlings.
The letter threatens to cut out Jon's heart, but the video notes Ramsay's favorite kind of execution is flaying, not cutting out hearts.

The video points out that another character once threatened to cut out Jon's heart -- a wildling who would care about Val and the baby, and the only character mentioned by name in the letter: Mance Rayder.
Mance Rayder is characterized in the books as a maverick, charismatic leader who uses cunning and deception to manipulate people.
In Book 1, when King Robert visits Winterfell, Mance is secretly at the feast -- he climbs the Wall and comes in disguise to spy.
In Book 3, Mance claims to have the Horn of Winter, a magic horn said to be able to destroy the Wall, but it turns out he lied about it to manipulate Jon.
Mance has history with Jon -- the first time they met, Mance warned Jon not to play tricks with him, but Jon later ends up betraying Mance by leaving the wildlings.
Mance has recently used a lot of the same language found in the Pink Letter -- he calls Melisandre a “red witch,” calls Jon “bastard,” and calls the Night's Watch “crows.”

spoken_quote: for all the world to see
paraphrase: Mance says he was burned “for all the world to see,” which the video compares to the Pink Letter's description of Mance being caged “for all the north to see.”

The video argues it's plausible for Mance to fake a letter from Ramsay: while infiltrating Winterfell disguised as the singer “Abel,” he spends time around the Boltons and could have learned enough about Ramsay's character to impersonate him.
Mance speaks with Theon, so he could have learned about Ramsay's relationship with “Reek” and with Jeyne.
The video speculates Mance could have stolen some pink wax and a raven to send the letter in a rush, which might explain why the Pink Letter's seal is only a “smear” of wax.
The video raises the difficult question of why Mance would write the letter at all -- even if he could predict it would make Jon ride south with wildlings, it's unclear how that helps Mance.
Mance doesn't want the wildlings to fight in Stannis's war, saying his “people have bled enough.”
verbatim: “Blood,” said Mance Rayder. “I’d win in the end, yes, but you’d bleed me, and my people have bled enough.”

paraphrase: The video argues that if Mance wanted help, impersonating Ramsay to provoke Jon into rallying wildlings would be a ridiculously convoluted way to ask for it.
It's unclear why Mance would ask for “Reek,” or why he would ask for Val and his own baby nephew, since that could only put them in danger.
The video notes Mance does seem to have some secret plan at Winterfell -- he investigates the Winterfell crypts.

The name “Abel” is an anagram of Bael, a legendary wildling said to have snuck into Winterfell and stolen a Stark girl (paralleling Mance sneaking into Winterfell to rescue “Arya”) and to have hidden in the Winterfell crypts -- leading the video to speculate Mance's plan might be to hide there too, possibly searching Stark graves for the real Horn of Winter, since he had previously searched for it in graves beyond the Wall.

Even if Stannis defeats the Freys with the beacon-and-lake trick, there would still be thousands of Bolton troops in one of the strongest castles in Westeros, so his army alone might not be enough -- giving him a motive to want Jon and the wildlings' help.

Stannis has asked for Jon's support before, but Jon said he's sworn to the Night's Watch, so asking directly hasn't worked -- the video suggests a fake letter threatening Jon, his sister, and Mance could motivate Jon and the wildlings to ride south and help Stannis.
This would also fit with Stannis's plan to make Jon Lord of Winterfell -- if Jon leaves the Watch anyway, he might as well take up Stannis's offer.

Stannis has captured a Bolton maester, who would know Ramsay's handwriting style and might have the wax and raven needed to send a forged letter; he has also captured Theon, who could tell him the details about Mance and “Reek” that appear in the letter.

Stannis's army is starving badly enough that they've started eating their own dead, so the video argues he doesn't have time to wait weeks or months for Jon and an army to travel south from the Wall in winter -- by then Stannis and his men would be dead.

It might have worked if Stannis sent the letter earlier, but earlier he didn't yet have Theon or the captured maester, so he wouldn't have known the details about Mance and Reek that the letter contains.

The video argues faking a letter just isn't Stannis's style -- pulling off a military maneuver like a false beacon is one thing, but impersonating Ramsay and emotionally manipulating Jon isn't something Stannis, who is described as terrible with people and emotions, would do.
If the letter's real purpose was to motivate Jon to come south, the video argues it would talk more about Arya and less about hostages and Mance.
The video asks why Stannis would demand “Reek” when Stannis already has Reek (Theon) himself, and Jon doesn't even know what a “Reek” is.
Melisandre is shown to have secret schemes -- she faked Mance's death, and sending him to infiltrate Winterfell was her idea.
Jon suspects Melisandre has some other secret plan involving Mance.
Some fans think Mance and Melisandre wrote the Pink Letter together, though the video says their motive for doing so is still unclear.
Melisandre has visions of the future in her fires and actually predicts the arrival of the Pink Letter before it comes.

The video speculates Melisandre might have foreseen that Jon would be stabbed to death and then resurrected by her magic in a way that would ultimately help save the world, and that she may have realized Jon, not Stannis, is the true chosen one -- motivating her to engineer the letter to get Jon killed so he could fulfill his destiny.
But Melisandre repeatedly warns Jon not to get stabbed, and as of the last time we see her, she still believes Stannis is the chosen one.
Barbrey Dustin, a new character introduced in Book 5, is mysterious -- she openly supports the Boltons but secretly hates Ramsay because he murdered her nephew.
Barbrey asks Theon to show her the Winterfell crypts around the same time Mance is asking about the crypts, leading some fans to think Mance and Barbrey might be working together.
There are also hints that Barbrey is working with Wyman Manderly's conspiracy, with some fans theorizing they wrote the Pink Letter to lure Jon south so he could become King in the North.

The video suggests most of this Barbrey/Manderly/crypt intrigue is probably unrelated to the Pink Letter itself, despite how much secret scheming is happening in the north (down to Jon secretly swapping the baby Aemon with Gilly's baby).

The video's overall conclusion is that, based on the text, it makes the most sense for Ramsay to have written the letter himself, since it doesn't just serve a plot purpose of provoking Jon into being stabbed, but also an emotional purpose -- forcing Jon to choose between family and duty, and cementing Ramsay as a hated enemy.
The video notes that if the Pink Letter turns out to be a fake tied to some obscure subplot, that would undercut the emotional weight of the book's ending gut-punch.
The video's final position leaves room for a twist where Stannis isn't really dead, since the text still gives strong hints that he will defeat the Freys using the false-beacon trap.
